content.full_text:
/
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
October 22,
1993
John B.
Bahorski
City Administrator
City of Calistoga
1232 Washington Street
Calistoga,
CA
94515
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
A—93—396
Dear Mr.
Bahorski:
This is in response to your request for confirmation of
telephone advice provided to you on October 12,
1993, regarding
the provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the “Act”)
1 pertaining
to gifts.
This letter confirms the advice I provided to you on
that date.
In our telephone conversation, you stated that you were the
appointed City Administrator for the City of Calistoga.
Recently,
the owner of a Health Spa in your jurisdiction provided two free
passes to you,
worth approximately $200.
You asked about your
responsibilities regarding receipt of the passes.
As we discussed, the receipt of gifts by a public official
has many ramifications.
First,
every public official must
disclose all of his or her economic interests that could
foreseeably be affected by the exercise of the official’s duties.
(Sections 81002(c),
87200—87313.)
Gifts received are disciosable
if the value of gifts received from the donor during the calendar
year is worth $50 or more.
(Section 87207.)
Regulation 18946.1(a)
provides thata pass or ticket which
provides one—time admission or access to facilities,
goods,
services,
or other incidental tangible or intangible benefits is
valued at the face value of the pass or ticket, provided that the
face value is a price that was,
or otherwise would have been,
offered to the general public.
A pass or ticket has no value
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000—18954.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807 •
(916) 322-5660


Our File No.
A-93-396
Page
2
unless it is ultimately used or unless the official transfers the
pass or ticket to another person.
Pursuant to Regulation 18946.1,
the gift of the Spa tickets would be reportable.
Moreover,
Section 87100 prohibits any public official at any
level of state or local government from making, participating in
making,
or attempting to use his or her official position to
influence a governmental decision if it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision will have a material financial effect on any
donor of, or any intermediary or agent for a donor,
of gifts
aggregating $250 or more in value provided to, received by, or
promised to the public official within 12 months prior to the time
when the decision is made.
(Section 87103(e).)
You stated that
the value is less than $250.
Consequently,
I advised that,
assuming you have not and will not receive additional gifts from
the Spa owner,
you would not have an economic interest in the
donor.
And finally,
the Act now provides gift limits with respect to
certain state and local public officials.
However, Section 89501,
which applies the limit to local officials,
only limits gifts and
honoraria to elected officers.
2
If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at
(916)
322—590l.
Sincerely,
Steven G.
Churchwell
General Counsel
ohW.We
Counsel, Legal Division
2
Section 89501 provides:
“No local elected officeholder shall
accept any honoraria for any speech given, article published,
or
attendance at any public or private conference,
convention,
meeting, social event, meal,
or like gathering,
or any gifts,
from
any single source, which is in excess of one thousand dollars
($1,000),
in any calendar year, except reimbursement for actual
travel expenses and reasonable subsistence in connection
therewith.”
Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries.
Alternatively,
copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of l0c per page.

sections.question: A City Administrator received two free passes to a Health Spa worth approximately $200 from the Spa owner in the jurisdiction. What are the responsibilities regarding receipt of these passes under the Political Reform Act?
sections.conclusion: Pursuant to Regulation 18946.1, the gift of the Spa tickets would be reportable. Assuming the official has not and will not receive additional gifts from the Spa owner, and assuming the value is less than $250, the official would not have an economic interest in the donor under Section 87100. The gift limit restrictions under Section 89501 apply only to elected officers, not appointed administrators.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether a city administrator must disclose and comply with gift reporting and conflict-of-interest provisions when receiving two free spa passes worth $200 from a business owner in the jurisdiction.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC confirmed that the spa passes are reportable gifts under Regulation 18946.1, but since the value is less than $250 and no additional gifts are expected, the official does not have a reportable economic interest in the donor under Section 87100. Gift limits under Section 89501 do not apply to appointed officials.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is a confirmed advice letter based on prior telephone consultation dated October 12, 1993. The question and conclusion sections are synthesized from the narrative body since the document lacks formal Q&A section headers. The letter cites relevant statutory sections (87200-87313, 87207, 87100, 87103(e), 89501) and regulations (18946.1).

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: A City Administrator received two free passes to a Health Spa worth approximately $200 from the Spa owner in the jurisdiction. What are the responsibilities regarding receipt of these passes under the Political Reform Act?

CONCLUSION: Pursuant to Regulation 18946.1, the gift of the Spa tickets would be reportable. Assuming the official has not and will not receive additional gifts from the Spa owner, and assuming the value is less than $250, the official would not have an economic interest in the donor under Section 87100. The gift limit restrictions under Section 89501 apply only to elected officers, not appointed administrators.

embedding.summary: This is an FPPC confirmation letter regarding the gift reporting obligations of an appointed City Administrator who received two free spa passes worth approximately $200. The FPPC advised that the passes are reportable gifts but do not trigger conflict-of-interest restrictions because the value is below $250.
